title: Towards a Green Economy in Uruguay: Stimulating Sustainable Production Practices and Low-Emission Technologies

sections.lessons_learned:
Biovalor's implementation generated a number of valuable technical and operational
lessons which can be applied to future projects both in Uruguay and in the global
portfolio of UNIDO-supported initiatives.
Regarding project design:
•
The lack of a clear objective definition may result in multiple and complex
objectives which creates unnecessary difficulties during implementation and the
subsequent evaluation of results.
•
The proper inclusion of private pilot and/or demonstration subprojects requires
an adequate definition of procedures, ideally in the form of a detailed Subproject
Manual produced during project preparation, and not during the initial phase of
implementation, to maximize the operation period of financed technologies.
50
•
The fulfillment of the project objectives should not be conditioned to the co-
financers’ decisions and actions, particularly in those cases where the execution
of the investment committed does not include a significant contribution of project
resources.
•
The requirement of the 10:1 co-financing ratio is significantly high, because of
this, it must be duly interpreted and evaluated by the preparation team, and
incorporated into the design differentiating the co-financers' commitment from
the potential contribution of the planned investments to the outcome indicators.
Regarding project implementation:
o   Institutional aspects : Biovalor demonstrated that effective inter-ministerial
coordination becomes crucial not only for an adequate governance, but also for
analyzing cross-cutting issues and generating stronger solutions agreed upon
by all parties. Also, in projects that combine regulatory and technology adoption
goals by the productive sector, the joint development of operational coordination
instruments with the academia, research activities, and the private sector is
essential to ensure the viability of the actions implemented.
o   Flexibility : In order to maintain actions relevant and consistent, some flexibility
in implementation becomes critical to allow for eventual adjustments to the
original design parameters. To achieve this, a technically qualified and
committed PMU is required, together with an active and representative
governance, and the active involvement of UNIDO. The unit should work closely
with the executing institution and the Steering Committee, strengthening
existing capacities in participating bodies and transmitting analytical,
operational and technical objectivity.
o   Management : The technical and operational functions of the PMU should be
duly complemented by an agile and decentralized system of administrative and
financial management, such as the one provided by the UNIDO Local
Representative team which optimized the use of time so that the Biovalor PMU
could focus on the project's technical execution.
o   Monitoring and follow-up : UNIDO’s contribution to the success of the project
would have been more effective, had the mid-term review been more
comprehensive and focused on adjustments to overcome difficulties and/or had
introduced new implementation activities during the remaining execution period.
Depending on the relevance and depth of the recommended adjustments and
modifications, restructuring the original project, including indicators and closing
dates, would have allowed for the formalization of agreed changes, a better
planning of actions, and a more realistic analysis of the final results.
Regarding pilot subprojects:
•
The beneficiaries’ motivation and profile represent a key factor and they should
be included in the subproject selection matrix.
•
Both during project design and implementation, it is essential to define whether
the subprojects to be supported pursue a pilot objective (experimentation of
new technologies) or a demonstration objective (promoting the adoption of
proven technologies).
51
•
In the case of demonstration subprojects, project support should further
promote a pre-existing intention of the beneficiary, which justifies a significant
amount of co-financing. The Biovalor experience showed that, beyond the
financial support received, the “moral”, technical and logistical support
provided throughout subproject implementation was highly appreciated by all
beneficiaries.
•
It is reasonable for some pilot subprojects not to produce satisfactory results
(e.g., the Tupambaé subproject, supported by Biovalor), but demonstrating
that an application or technology is not viable can also be considered a lesson
learnt and a positive outcome for the project.
•
The pilot cases also showed that there are circular economy projects in
agriculture that can offer economically viable solutions, especially if current
regulations set high costs for traditional waste disposal methods.
•
The effective contribution of pilot projects to the project objectives requires a
realistic execution schedule, with enough time for the implementation and
demonstration of the proven technology, and for the technical and economic
evaluation of its results. In practical terms and according to the experience
gained, subproject selection, design and installation should not extend beyond
the first 30 months of implementation.
•
In the exceptional and unforeseen event that the start-up of a pilot subproject
is significantly delayed, it is necessary to establish monitoring mechanisms
and, after project completion, disseminate the lessons learnt.
The implementation of Biovalor demonstrated that the reduction of GHG and waste
achieved through the use of these technologies generates, in turn, multiple potential
benefits in terms of environmental protection and product access to international
markets. Even though these benefits are difficult to estimate, they should be
considered by national authorities when defining appropriate regulations and
instruments, and by international agencies to comprehensively assess the outcome
and impact of projects with environmentally positive interventions. In the case of
Biovalor, reducing the degradation of aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems contributes to
maintaining their ecosystem services, including the provision of drinking water, the
quality of water resources, net primary production, species habitats, etc. Therefore, the
initial and final cost-benefit analysis of this kind of projects should also include an
estimate of the value of maintaining such environmental and commercial services
(through avoided water treatment costs, avoided losses in product sales, in tourism
activities, and in agricultural production due to soil degradation).
52

30 November 2020

45 working days over the period
ORGANIZATIONAL CONTEXT
The UNIDO Independent Evaluation Division (ODG/EIO/IED) is responsible for the independent
evaluation function of UNIDO. It supports learning, continuous improvement and accountability, and
provides factual information about result and practices that feed into the programmatic and strategic
decision-making processes. Evaluation is an assessment, as systematic and impartial as possible, of a
programme, a project or a theme. Independent evaluations provide evidence-based information that
is credible, reliable and useful, enabling the timely incorporation of findings, recommendations and
lessons learnt into the decision- making processes at organization-wide, programme and project level.
ODG/EIO/IED is guided by the UNIDO Evaluation Policy, which is aligned to the norms and standards
for evaluation in the UN system.
PROJECT CONTEXT
Detailed background information of the project can be found the terms of reference (TOR) for the
terminal evaluation.
The National consultant/team leader will evaluate the project in accordance with the evaluation-
related terms of reference (TOR). He/she will perform, inter alia, the following main tasks:

sections.conclusions:
The Biovalor project is considered  highly relevant  to Uruguay and fully  consistent
with the country’s existing policies and regulatory framework. To a large extent thanks
to the effective institutional articulation achieved, the efficient management of the
Project Management Unit (PMU), and the exemplary support provided by UNIDO's
local representation, the project contributed to reducing the main barriers identified in
the theory of change and showed the necessary flexibility to adapt to the changing
conditions experienced during execution as a commendable feature
Despite its complexity, the execution stage succeeded in  meeting the objectives set ,
reducing GHG emissions and significantly improving waste management, promoting a
shift towards environmentally sustainable development and, in particular, towards a
circular, low-emission economy model.
The fulfilment of project objectives was achieved through the synergies and
coordinated implementation of its five  components . Specifically, the project
strengthened environmental policies, the regulatory framework and the knowledge
base on waste conversion and valorization, fostered private sector investments in
technologies relevant to the project's objective, developed capacities (both within
academia and the public and private sectors), and devoted considerable efforts to
communicate and disseminate the knowledge gained.
Through the implementation of pilot subprojects, four specific technologies
(composting, anaerobic digestion, nutrient circularity, and biofuels) were tested on
existing commercial operations with satisfactory results. Although not considered
sophisticated, these technologies were appropriate and innovative for the development
stage of waste valorization in the country. As such, these subprojects provided
concrete examples of success, showing the technical and economic feasibility of
adopting the technologies tested.
In terms of  sustainability , the evaluation conducted makes it possible to conclude that
reasonable conditions exist for the various contributions of Biovalor to be supported
after project completion, therefore generating a solid basis for achieving positive long-
term impacts. The potential impact resulting from the project depends to a large extent
on the sustainability of the instruments developed or promoted for the consolidation
and possible expansion of Biovalor actions within public sector priorities and the
subsequent adoption of these practices by the private sector. For this to materialize,
active public interventions will be needed to continue the development of policies,
instruments, and knowledge. As well as to promote in the private sector the replication
of successful experiences to obtain results in biogas generation on dairy farms, waste
processing in small urban areas, commercial development of composting, and the
generation of alternative fuels from the meat-processing industry.
Regarding the project’s  potential impact , the significant progress made by the project
in terms of regulatory framework, institutional development and technological
demonstration suggest that the implementation of Biovalor should have a significant
impact on Uruguay's environmental performance, mainly through the adoption of
48
sustainable models of low-carbon production and waste processing. This conclusion
rests on the fact that the project was inserted into an existing state policy, the 2016
National Environmental System, which aimed to address eutrophication pollution in the
country’s main watercourses and to promote the coordination between productive
sectors and environmental protection, water management and climate change efforts.
To the extent that the validation, expansion and adoption of technologies for effluent
and waste management and utilization is duly supported and promoted by the
competent institutions, the Biovalor experience will become a decisive factor in the
effective mitigation of priority environmental problems for Uruguay and the fulfillment
of its global commitments.
In operational terms, regardless of the good results achieved, there were some design
flaws that influenced to a greater or lesser extent the effectiveness and efficiency of its
execution. The complexity of the objective set, the absence of a specific waste
transformation goal, a certain lack of realism regarding the deadlines for
implementation and a particular interpretation and operationalization of the concept of
co-financing are highlighted.
In terms of implementation, there are some opportunities for improvement, among
them, the low degree of execution in the first years of the project, the lack of concrete
actions in some sectors of high waste generation (feed lot and dairy industry), the
limited dissemination of calls for expression of interest to pilot projects and their
subsequent late implementation (e.g., Rincón Blanco) where possibilities of evaluating
the different technologies tested and disseminating them to other potential
stakeholders were significantly limited.
In short, the project was Satisfactory, exceeded expectations in several of the goals
set for each component and generated several positive results, with good prospects
for future support and expansion, despite limitations observed in its design and some
other operational constraints.
sections.recommendations:
o  The Uruguayan government should continue promoting instruments to
encourage the replication of Biovalor’s successful experiences, especially the
generation and use of biogas, nutrient circularity on dairy farms, the use of
waste in meat processing plants and the installation of micro biodigesters in
public institutions in urban centers.
o  Similarly, it is necessary to define and develop the potential to generate even
greater environmental benefits through the promotion of waste processing
technologies in other important production chains, such as industrial dairy
processing plants, feed lots, and poultry and pig farming.
o  While significant progress has been made through the project, it is crucial that
the MIEM, MGAP and DINAMA continue their joint efforts to eliminate the
barriers to competitiveness and expansion of some of the technologies tested,
such as the lack of incentives for manufacturers and suppliers for equipment
and inputs linked to project technologies, and the tax burden on compost
marketing.
o  The environmental authority, together with the Ministry of Economy and
Finance, should seek further dissemination of COMAP as an incentive
49
instrument for private investment aimed at waste processing and
decarbonization of production activities.
o  Similarly, an inter-institutional effort is required to develop a technology and tax
framework that promotes the viability and competitiveness of bio-products,
including the development of appropriate technologies and the expansion of tax
benefits for SMEs and small farmers contributing to IMESI.
o  ANDE, in coordination with Biovalor’s executing bodies, should make the
necessary efforts to ensure the continuity of the Circular Opportunities program
and to obtain a budget allocation similar to that of 2019, in line with the demands
of the private sector.
o  With regard to gender, these aspects should be more explicitly mentioned in
future projects indicators.
o  Because it was not possible to complete its installation before the end of
Biovalor, the pilot project of Rincón Blanco biodigester should receive an active
monitoring by DINAMA to ensure its proper completion within the agreed
deadline (30 June, 2021). Similarly, appropriate monitoring of financed nutrient-
circularity initiatives by academic and research institutions is needed to ensure
result generation and dissemination.
o  It would be important to incorporate the lessons learnt from the project and to
allocate resources for specific actions to consolidate Biovalor’s results in the
design of the new GEF-financed project managed by UNIDO in Uruguay.
o  In terms of project design and management, UNIDO should assess the
possibility of (i) reviewing the methodology for recruiting and including co-
financers into the project’s costs and results matrix (in compliance with GEF
requirements); (ii) providing additional flexibility for procurement procedures for
private pilot/demonstration projects; (iii) expanding the scope of the mid-term
review to identify possible restructuring needs of activities, budget allocation
and indicators; and (iv) reviewing project management criteria and
responsibilities, making the necessary adjustments to achieve greater
decentralization of project management and monitoring functions.
sections.results:
development of policy and regulatory instruments , that contributed to improving
the process of policy development, adoption and regulation through better institutional
coordination, awareness raising, and knowledge generation.
In terms of joint actions and institutional coordination, two concrete initiatives actively
promoted by the project stand out:
o  The creation of the  Circular Economy Network,  established by Biovalor in
collaboration with Transforma Uruguay, consisting of a significant number of
public, private and academic sector leading organizations, including 15 state
institutions and agencies, 7 universities, 6 civil society organizations, and 3
farmer organizations (output 4.3).
o  The alignment of the project with  the Plan of Action for the Environmental
Protection of the Santa Lucia River Basin , launched in 2015 aiming to reduce
pollution of the main source of drinking water for Montevideo, the largest urban
center in the country. Along this line, new actions were established to reduce
effluents discharged to the water courses by dairy farms in the basin. This
support was aimed at developing best practices and technological
developments, and promoting nutrient circularity. The project significantly
contributed to this objective, not only through the pilot projects, but also through
the financial support given to the research project on nutrient circularity on dairy
farms (described in the next section), and in particular by coordinating with the
MGAP and DINAMA in the implementation of the package of measures by
MGAP with World Bank funding; this resulted in technical and financial support
to more than 400 dairy producers in the Santa Lucia Basin to improve effluent
management. This initiative is also considered relevant from the point of view
of counterpart contributions not provided for in the project design, which partly
23
contributed to compensating unimplemented investments (the effectiveness
section of this report contains more information on this).
In terms of the development of policy and regulatory instruments, the project made
significant contributions, which allowed to strengthen the set of public actions to
promote an adequate regulatory framework for sustainable low-carbon production. In
this regard, the promotion and adoption of the circular economy are considered to be
the most significant contributions made by Biovalor.
Within the context of component 1 (Strengthening of policies and regulatory
framework), the design of Biovalor included the development of an Action Plan to
remove financial barriers to low-emission waste valorization (output 1.3). The Plan
(Transforma Uruguay, 2019) was specifically focused on the size of the circular
economy and was led by Transforma Uruguay (which no longer exists). Actions
prioritized by the Plan included the industry transition to the circular economy; the
design of a technology center in circular bioeconomics; the promotion of nutrient
circularity on dairy farms; the strengthening of circular economy capacities; and waste
volirazation.
In response to the guidelines of the Plan, Biovalor implemented two specific actions
considered of great significance for the results of the project: the organization of the
First Circular Economy Forum in Uruguay, and the subsequent elaboration and
implementation of the Circular Opportunities program.
The  First Circular Economy Forum  was held in Montevideo in September 2017 and
featured 25 speakers from 14 countries and more than 350 attendees. It is interesting
to note that, as part of the organization of the forum, the project conducted a national
survey that identified more than 700 local circular economy leaders in the public,
private and academic sectors.
The opinions gathered during the interviews unanimously agree that the Forum
represented a fundamental milestone for the project and for the process of introducing
the concept of circular economy in Uruguay, not only because of the level of awareness
generated but also because it was instrumental in creating strategic alliances aimed at
promoting knowledge and technological innovation, in addition to identifying specific
opportunities that would enable the business sector to consider circular alternatives in
its operations.
The Circular Opportunities program  was created in 2018 thanks to a cooperation
agreement, generated by the Forum, between Biovalor and the National Development
Agency (ANDE). Applying competitive and demand-based procedures, the program
was designed to provide technical and financial support to projects incorporating
circular economy technologies. Based on periodic dissemination/motivational calls and
events, those ideas assessed as eligible receive support on three specific phases of
the productive innovation process for the incorporation of circular technology: (i)
validation of ideas; (ii) implementation and prototypes; and (iii) implementation of
projects.
The first call for proposals was made in 2018 with a budget of US$ 575,000; a
contribution of US$ 275,000 by Biovalor and US$ 300,000 by ANDE. In 2019, the
24
second call was exclusively financed by ANDE, which allocated a budget of US$ 1.0
million. Between 2018 and 2019, through a joint project evaluation and monitoring
committee, composed of Biovalor/UNIDO, ANDE and the ministries represented in
Biovalor, 48 ideas and projects were selected for technical and financial support for
US$ 1.5 million, with an estimated counterpart contribution from the beneficiaries of
US$ 2.0 million.
The program remained in force during 2020, also with funding from ANDE and support
from other institutions and initiatives, such as PAGE, and Biovalor involvement in the
selection and monitoring of projects. The budgetary constraints imposed by the
pandemic forced ANDE to significantly reduce the amount allocated to the program,
so it was only possible to support ideas validation proposals.
The following are examples of projects supported by the program:
i.
Organic brick production
ii.
Poultry slaughterhouses waste processing
iii.
Production and marketing of Tannat grape marc flour
iv.
Valorization of sheep leather
v.
Thermoacoustic eco-insulation from recycled cellulose.
vi.
Manufacture of furniture from recycled wind turbine blades
vii.
Bio-manufacturing of materials from fungi
viii.
Bioconversion of malt bagasse for poultry feed
ix.
Lithium battery management and recycling
x.
Recycling of silo bags for the manufacture of waste bags
While this line of work had not been explicitly planned during the project design phase,
Biovalor’s support to the promotion of circular initiatives was of great importance. It
was one of the main contributions of the project to addressing the country's
environmental priorities.
In terms of continuity, interviews with ANDE authorities highlighted Biovalor’s
contribution to the program’s creation and the growing demand from the private sector
for this type of support, justifying the institution’s decision to continue the program with
its own resources, after Biovalor’s completion.
In addition to the creation and institutionalization of the Circular Opportunities program,
the implementation of the project made it possible to make significant progress in
relation to the regulatory framework to enable the adoption by the productive sector of
technologies related to the project’s objectives.
In particular, the project technical team worked actively to improve the technical and
economic conditions for the development of a compost market. These valuable
contributions represented a complement to the three pilot projects implemented that
included waste composting (Tresor, BioTerra and Dorados del Sol), and consisted of
technical work and the support by the MGAP before the General Directorate for
Agricultural Services (DGSA) to develop the regulatory framework required to generate
competitive conditions for the production and marketing of compost as an organic input
for agricultural use, allowing the use of organic waste with the consequent reduction
of GHG emissions. More specifically, the work led by Biovalor’s PMU with contributions
25
from public and academic institutions, allowed the definition, characterization,
registration, and control for the commercialization of organic products for agricultural
use.
Biovalor’s work also included the difficult task of improving public policies to financially
encourage the adoption of technologies for waste transformation and valorization.
Considering that, for the most part, these technologies require higher investments than
conventional processes, the development of public instruments that would allow the
investor to absorb their incremental costs was a priority for the sustainable adoption
and/or replication of these technologies.
In this regard, it is important to highlight the project’s contribution to the dissemination
of the opportunities provided by the Investment Law (COMAP) regarding exemption
from IRAE taxation and to the enactment of regulations that allow industrial or
agricultural companies to reduce VAT in the purchase of machinery that mitigates or
eliminates negative environmental impacts, both of great importance for investments
in waste transformation and/or valorization.
In other cases, Biovalor's considerable efforts on this issue were not sufficient to
generate financial, credit or tax incentives. Some examples are the proposals for (i)
interest rate reduction on bank loans for “green” investments, (ii) differentiated prices
for energy produced from biogas; (iii) VAT exemption for agricultural compost; and (iv)
adaptation of tax benefits for large companies to the special tax regime governing small
and medium-sized enterprises (IMESI).
In short, while it is necessary to continue to develop appropriate instruments of
financial incentives, the actions implemented by Biovalor have allowed to combine
greater knowledge, improvements in the regulatory framework, and the adoption of
innovative incentive and promotion instruments such as Circular Opportunities directly
linked to the results achieved in waste transformation and emission reduction. These
achievements demonstrate the interest and commitment of all the sectors involved and
represent the fundamental pillars to develop an economic and environmentally
sustainable model of low-emission production in Uruguay.
A suitable system of techology development and transfer.
Project activities directly linked to this objective effectively started during the second
half of the project. Undoubtedly, these delays had a negative effect on the possibility
of the project to contribute to the functioning of a suitable system of technology
development and transfer. In spite of the limited scope of the activities supported by
Biovalor in this regard, the efforts made to incorporate the issues of waste use and
valorization, and nutrient circularity in the academic field and the areas of research and
technological transfer have been valuable and, to the extent that they are properly
applied and continued after project completion, they have the potential to contribute to
the development, adoption and consolidation of sustainable productive systems.
Beyond the collaboration of the academia in providing knowledge to support all
technical activities, Biovalor’s articulated work with the academic sector also enabled
specific outputs that accelerated the incorporation of the topics of circular economy
and waste valorization in training and research programs, created information and
26
knowledge networks for public and private decision-making, and strengthened the
technical structures responsible for developing specific support instruments for the
private sector, particularly at MGAP, DINAMA and MIEM.
In terms of academic activity, the training courses and the thesis program that received
technical and financial contributions from the project are highlighted (output 4.5). More
specifically, Biovalor supported the organization of four training c
